title: The Neuroscience of Presence and How Psychedelics Anchor You in the Now
body:
The Neuroscience of Presence and How Psychedelics Anchor You in the Now

1. Opening Hook - The Moment I Lost and Found Time

I still remember the first time I truly felt present. Not just the mental chatter quieting down, but a deep, visceral sense of “now” flowing through every fiber of my being. It wasn’t during meditation or a fancy retreat - it was on a rainy afternoon, sitting quietly by a window, sipping tea. The world outside was gray, dripping, muffled, and the familiar knot of anxiety that usually buzzed in my chest had vanished. Time felt different. It didn’t tick forward or slip away - it stretched and folded, like a ribbon I could hold gently in my hands.

Years later, I would discover that the feeling I stumbled upon that day was what many call presence, or mindful awareness. It’s the ability to fully inhabit a moment, untethered from ruminations about past mistakes or worries over what’s next. Of course, holding onto that feeling is tough. The mind is a relentless storyteller, always pulling me out of this peaceful now, dragging me into yesterday’s regrets or tomorrow’s fears.

Then came psychedelics - a medicine that taught me presence in ways I hadn’t expected. Under their influence, the grip of time loosens, the noisy ego softens, and the denseness of experience shifts. Psychedelics don’t just show you presence; they anchor you in it, moment to moment. They don’t promise to fix your life; they offer a glimpse into how life unfolds when you’re fully here, fully awake.

But how does this happen in the brain? What neuroscience explains this strange, beautiful anchoring of consciousness in the present? And how can we, beyond those intense sessions, learn to carry that presence into ordinary days? These are questions I’ve been chasing for years. It’s a journey weaving together personal experience, scientific research, and the wisdom of psychedelic pioneers like Stanislav Grof, Roland Griffiths, and Rick Strassman.

Presence isn’t just a feel-good state. It’s tied deeply to how our brain constructs reality, processes emotions, and even heals trauma. Understanding this can help us see why psychedelics feel so grounding, paradoxically, even when they dissolve the boundaries of self. So let’s start there - with the brain itself, and how it makes sense of now.

2. The Brain and Presence: A Scientific Exploration

Presence is often described as being “in the moment,” but what does that mean neurologically? At its core, presence involves the brain’s ability to filter out distractions - internal and external - and to engage fully with whatever is happening right now. Neuroscientists point to a few key players in this process.

One critical area is the default mode network (DMN), a set of brain regions active during self-referential thinking, mind-wandering, and rumination. When the DMN is overactive, people tend to get stuck in loops of negative thoughts about the past or future - a major barrier to presence. Conversely, when the DMN quiets down, there’s more room for sensory experience and direct engagement with the present moment.

Dr. Robin Carhart-Harris, a leading figure in psychedelic neuroscience, showed that psychedelics reduce activity in the DMN, temporarily breaking the habitual patterns that keep us trapped in narrative self-focus. This helps explain why psychedelic experiences often feel like a freeing of consciousness, allowing people to step out of their mental ruts and connect deeply with their surroundings.

But presence isn’t just about less thinking. It’s also about enhanced sensory processing and emotional regulation. The prefrontal cortex, involved in attention and executive function, plays a significant role here. Under psychedelics, the connectivity between the prefrontal cortex and sensory areas changes, sometimes increasing the brain’s sensitivity to sights, sounds, touch, and even interoception - the sense of the body’s internal state.

Interestingly, the insula, a region linked to interoception and emotional awareness, lights up during moments of deep presence. The insula helps bridge the mind and body, anchoring awareness in bodily sensations. That’s why practices like mindfulness, yoga, or mushroom supplementation with compounds like Lion’s Mane (which supports neural plasticity) can enhance the capacity to stay present by tuning the brain’s internal radar.

All of this points to presence as a dynamic interplay between decreased self-referential thought, heightened sensory engagement, and embodied awareness. Psychedelics act like a neurochemical reset switch that loosens rigid networks and opens space for this new kind of awake, anchored experience.

3. Research and Evidence: What the Studies Reveal

Science has come a long way from dismissing psychedelics as mere party drugs. pioneering research, especially in the past two decades, has uncovered how these substances influence brain networks tied to presence and well-being.

One landmark study by Dr. Roland Griffiths and his team at Johns Hopkins was key. Using psilocybin - the active molecule in magic mushrooms - they showed that participants often experienced “mystical-type experiences” characterized by timelessness and unity, both closely linked to presence. Griffiths found that these states predicted long-lasting positive changes in mood and behavior. The participants’ ability to disengage from their usual self-bound narratives aligned with decreased DMN activity observed in neuroimaging studies.

Further evidence comes from Carhart-Harris’s experiments at Imperial College London. Using fMRI scans, his team observed that psychedelics cause “desegregation” of brain networks, meaning regions that don’t usually communicate start interacting in novel ways. This rewiring correlates with heightened sensory experience and the dissolution of the ego barrier, both of which contribute to a vivid sense of presence.

But it’s not just the acute psychedelic session that matters. Longitudinal studies show that the integration phase - the weeks and months after the experience - is crucial for anchoring presence. Psychedelics can catalyze neuroplasticity, supported by increased brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), which opens a window for lasting change in habits, mood, and cognition.

Stanislav Grof’s early work in the 1970s with LSD psychotherapy highlighted presence’s role in trauma healing, underscoring how being in the moment allows for deep emotional processing. More recently, the connection between presence and trauma resolution has been explored by Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, whose book The Body Keeps the Score explains how trauma can hijack presence, locking people in the past.

Finally, the concept of microdosing and its effects on everyday presence is gaining traction. Studies suggest that low doses of psychedelics can improve attention, mood, and sensory sensitivity without overwhelming the user, potentially increasing mindfulness in daily life. This is a space where mushroom supplements, including Lion’s Mane, support brain health and cognitive function. Products like Host Defense Lion's Mane Capsules are becoming popular for their nootropic benefits, complementing psychedelic practices with natural neuro-support.

4. How to Anchor Yourself in Presence with Psychedelic Insights

Understanding the neuroscience is one thing, but how do you bring presence into your own life? Psychedelics offer a powerful doorway to experiencing presence directly, but the real work begins before and after the journey itself.

First, preparation matters. Setting an intention around presence can orient the experience toward awareness instead of distraction or escape. I recommend reading Michael Pollan’s How to Change Your Mind before your first session. It’s one of the clearest guides to what psychedelics do and how to approach them with respect and curiosity.

During the psychedelic session, try to notice sensory details without judgment. The colors, textures, sounds around you - they become entry points back into the moment when the mind threatens to spiral. Breathing deeply and anchoring in the body, perhaps through gentle movement or focused attention on bodily sensations, helps engage the insula and further grounds presence.

After the experience, integration is key. I’ve seen too many people rush back to daily routines without sitting with what they learned about presence. Journaling, meditation, and bodywork can extend that anchored awareness. Building daily rituals that cultivate mindfulness - whether it’s a morning tea ritual, mindful walking, or breath-focused moments - can keep presence alive long after the trip ends.

Adding mushroom supplements like Host Defense Lion's Mane Capsules can support cognitive clarity and neuroplasticity, helping your brain retain those shifts of attention and awareness.

Consider also the role of community and safe spaces. Talking about your psychedelic insights with trusted friends or guides can reinforce presence, helping you notice when your mind drifts and gently inviting it back. Presence isn’t about perfection; it’s a practice of showing up again and again, with kindness and curiosity.

5. Challenges on the Path to Presence and How to Face Them

Presence isn’t a default mode; it’s hard work, especially if mental patterns are deeply ingrained. The mind loves to pull us into stories - fears, regrets, fantasies - because they feel familiar, even if they cause suffering. Psychedelic experiences can crack open that loop, but the cracks aren’t always comfortable.

One common challenge is the resurgence of difficult emotions during or after a psychedelic session. When presence reveals pain or trauma, the knee-jerk reaction can be to avoid or suppress it. But turning away from discomfort only strengthens the mental resistance to presence. I’ve learned through my own practice and from therapists that leaning into these feelings with compassion, and with tools like grounding exercises or somatic therapy, can help you move through resistance and deepen presence.

Another obstacle is cognitive confusion. Psychedelics sometimes loosen the mind so much it wanders into chaos, making presence feel slippery or out of reach. This is where having a sitter or guide, and using simple anchors like breath or sensory contact, becomes invaluable. Reminding yourself that the mind’s nature is to wander can reduce frustration and make the journey less volatile.

Post-experience integration can also be tricky. Without support, insights about presence can fade or feel disconnected from daily realities. Sometimes, life circumstances - work stress, family demands, mental health struggles - pull you back into autopilot. Building habits slowly, with patience, is critical. Even a few minutes of morning awareness or a simple body scan before sleep can build resilience against slipping into distraction.

If trauma underlies your resistance, working with trauma-informed therapists or practitioners familiar with psychedelic integration is worth considering. Books like The Body Keeps the Score offer excellent frameworks for understanding how trauma blocks presence at a neurological and bodily level, and how healing unfolds.

The key is to remember that presence is a muscle. It strains, it slips, and sometimes it grows stronger with the struggle. Psychedelics can be a guide, but the real courage is in showing up consistently, even when it feels hard or messy.

6. Weaving Presence into Your Daily Life Over Time

Long after the psychedelic experience fades, presence is the thread that can keep your life feeling fresh, alive, and grounded. Cultivating presence day-to-day is less about trying to maintain an elevated state and more about creating gentle openings within your habitual flow.

One practice I’ve leaned on is mindful routine. Whether it’s brushing teeth, washing dishes, or walking the dog, these mundane actions can become portals to the present moment when attention is fully engaged. Psychedelic sessions expand your capacity to notice subtle experiences, but the real magic happens when you bring that noticing into everyday tasks.

Another layer is body awareness. Presence often lives in the body - the steady beat of the heart, the rhythm of breathing, the weight of your feet on the ground. Developing somatic awareness through yoga, dance, or simple breathwork helps maintain that embodied presence. Supplements like Host Defense Lion's Mane Capsules may support this process by promoting brain plasticity and cognitive function, making it easier for your brain to stay flexible and alert.

Community connection is equally important. Presence is easier to sustain when you’re with people who encourage openness and attentiveness. Sharing stories, supporting others’ growth, and feeling seen can break isolation and ground you in shared reality.

Lastly, patience is essential. Presence waxes and wanes. There will be days when your mind races or sadness pulls you under. Psychedelic experiences remind us that even those moments are part of life’s flow - not enemies to presence but aspects to greet with acceptance.

Life is not about perfect mindfulness but about returning to presence, again and again, with tenderness toward yourself. In this way, presence becomes less of a goal and more of a way of moving through the world - a calm current beneath the turbulence.

7. A Closing Thought to Sit With

Here’s something I’ve come to believe: presence is both simplest and most mysterious on the deepest level. It’s the place where time collapses, where the usual stories dissolve, and where you taste what it means to truly be. Psychedelics, in their strange, beautiful way, offer a door to that experience, reminding us that presence is not a distant ideal but the very ground of life itself.

And yet, presence is not something you can hold tightly. Like trying to catch a fish with your bare hands, it slips through grasping. The more you chase it, the more elusive it becomes. Instead, presence asks for a gentle surrender - a willingness to rest into what is, without trying to change or fix. It asks us to trust that existence is enough, that the moment unfolding right now is the whole story.

This is why the neuroscience of presence is so fascinating. It reveals that presence lives in the shifting dance of brain networks, in the quieting of self-boundaries, in the marriage of mind and body. It also shows that presence is a skill shaped by practice, by medicine, by relationship, and by courage.

So the next time you feel pulled away by your thoughts or overwhelmed by your feelings, remember the psychedelic insight: presence is not about having perfect control. It’s about leaning into the mystery of now, with your senses wide open, your mind softening, and your heart awake.

And maybe that’s enough - for this moment, and the next.
